feed.title: Iro AI Blog
feed.subtitle: Short, practical writing on AI fluency, prompting, AI tools, and the active-practice method.

entry.title: How to actually learn ChatGPT in 2026
entry.summary: The active-practice method that beats endless video courses.

entry.title: The 7 prompt patterns that work everywhere
entry.summary: Transferable structures for ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini, and Perplexity.

entry.title: How to spot AI hallucinations in 5 seconds
entry.summary: Quick checks that catch most mistakes before you trust them.

entry.title: AI agents, explained without the jargon
entry.summary: What agents actually are, when to use them, and where they fail.

entry.title: The best free AI tools for daily work in 2026
entry.summary: A practical stack across writing, research, coding, and visuals.

entry.title: AI for beginners: a 30-day learning plan
entry.summary: Four weeks from zero to genuinely useful — five minutes a day.

entry.title: ChatGPT vs Claude vs Gemini: which should you use in 2026?
entry.summary: A practical, no-hype comparison — and why the right answer is usually more than one.

entry.title: Why your AI prompts aren't working (and how to fix them)
entry.summary: Five reasons prompts fail — and the quick fixes that turn vague output into useful work.

entry.title: AI automation for beginners: 5 things to automate this week
entry.summary: No code, no agents — just five repetitive tasks you can hand to AI starting today.

entry.title: Iro AI vs Coursiv: which AI learning app is right for you in 2026?
entry.summary: Both teach you to use AI in a few minutes a day. The real difference is how you learn — and how you pay.

entry.title: Iro AI vs Learnova: which AI learning app should you pick in 2026?
entry.summary: Two mobile apps that teach you AI tools — but one has you reading lessons and one has you practicing. Here's how to choose.

entry.title: Iro AI vs Brilliant: which should you use to actually get good at AI?
entry.summary: Both are gamified, both are excellent at what they do — but they teach two very different things. Here's how to pick.

entry.title: Iro AI vs Duolingo: the "Duolingo for AI," explained
entry.summary: People call Iro "the Duolingo for AI" for a reason — same addictive method, completely different subject. Here's how they compare.

entry.title: What is AI fluency? A practical definition (and how to build it)
entry.summary: AI fluency is the practical ability to use, direct, and judge AI tools well enough to get reliably better results than you would without them. Here's what it actually means — and how to build it in a few minutes a day.

entry.title: What is prompt engineering? A clear definition with examples
entry.summary: Prompt engineering is the skill of writing and structuring instructions so an AI model reliably gives you the output you actually want. Here's what it means, why it works, and how to get good at it — with before-and-after examples.

entry.title: ChatGPT vs Claude: which should you use in 2026?
entry.summary: ChatGPT and Claude are the two best general-purpose AI assistants in 2026 — and the right pick depends on what you do most. Here's an honest, side-by-side breakdown.

entry.title: How to use AI for studying (without cheating yourself)
entry.summary: Used well, AI is the best study partner ever invented — it explains, quizzes, and plans around you. Used lazily, it quietly robs you of the learning. Here's how to get the upside.

entry.title: How to learn AI without coding (a no-code path for 2026)
entry.summary: You do not need to code to learn AI. Using AI tools well is a language-and-judgment skill, not a programming one. Here is the no-code path — what to learn, in what order, and how to practice it in a few minutes a day.

entry.title: What is an LLM? Large language models explained simply
entry.summary: A large language model (LLM) is an AI trained on huge amounts of text to predict the next word — which lets it answer, write, summarize, and reason in plain language. Here's how LLMs work, what they're good and bad at, and how to use them well.

entry.title: How long does it take to learn AI? A realistic timeline
entry.summary: Learning to use AI is faster than most people expect: with about five minutes of focused practice a day, you can be genuinely useful in two to three weeks. Here's a realistic timeline — and why building AI takes far longer than using it.

entry.title: The 7 AI skills worth learning in 2026
entry.summary: The most valuable AI skill in 2026 isn't any single tool — it's the ability to direct, judge, and apply AI to real work. Here are the seven skills that matter most, why they're durable, and how to build each one.

entry.title: 15 ChatGPT prompts for beginners (copy, paste, and learn)
entry.summary: The best ChatGPT prompts for beginners are simple templates you can copy, paste, and adapt. Here are 15 across work, learning, and everyday life — plus the one pattern that makes any prompt better.

entry.title: AI agents vs. automation: what's the difference (and when to use each)?
entry.summary: Automation follows fixed rules you define; an AI agent decides its own steps toward a goal. Knowing which one to reach for saves time and avoids expensive surprises. Here's the difference in plain English, with examples.

entry.title: How to use AI at work (a practical guide for 2026)
entry.summary: The best way to use AI at work is to start with a few high-value, low-risk tasks — drafting, summarizing, and research — verify everything that matters, and follow your company's rules. Here's where to start and how to do it responsibly.

entry.title: ChatGPT vs Gemini: which should you use in 2026?
entry.summary: ChatGPT is the more versatile all-rounder; Gemini is the better choice if your work lives inside Google. Here's the honest head-to-head — strengths, free tiers, and the best pick for each kind of task.

entry.title: The best AI apps in 2026 (and what each is best for)
entry.summary: The best AI app depends on the job: ChatGPT for everyday use, Claude for writing and code, Gemini inside Google, Perplexity for research, and Iro for actually getting good at all of them. Here's the shortlist.

entry.title: What is generative AI? A simple explanation with examples
entry.summary: Generative AI is artificial intelligence that creates new content — text, images, audio, video, or code — in response to a prompt. Here's how it works, what it can and can't do, and how to use it well.

entry.title: What is agentic AI? Agents explained simply (2026)
entry.summary: Agentic AI is AI that pursues a goal on its own — planning steps, using tools, and adapting — instead of just answering one prompt at a time. Here's what it means, how it differs from chatbots and generative AI, and when it's actually useful.

entry.title: How to write a good AI prompt: a step-by-step guide
entry.summary: A good prompt has four parts: context, role, task, and format. Add those, then refine the first answer. Here's the simple recipe, with before-and-after examples you can copy.

entry.title: The AI Fluency Pyramid: the 5 levels of getting good at AI
entry.summary: Most people get stuck on the bottom two levels of using AI and never reach the top, where the real leverage is. The AI Fluency Pyramid maps the five levels — from simply having access to fully integrating AI into your work — so you can see where you are and what to climb next.

entry.title: Claude vs Gemini: which should you use in 2026?
entry.summary: Claude is the better pick for serious writing, coding, and long documents; Gemini wins if your work lives in Google and for generous free multimodal use. Here's the honest head-to-head.

entry.title: ChatGPT vs Perplexity: which should you use in 2026?
entry.summary: ChatGPT is a versatile assistant for creating and doing; Perplexity is an answer engine for research with citations. They're built for different jobs — and many people use both. Here's how to choose.

entry.title: Will AI take your job? An honest answer (and how to stay ahead)
entry.summary: AI is unlikely to take your whole job soon — but it will change most jobs, and people who use AI well will outcompete those who don't. Here's the honest picture and how to future-proof yourself.

entry.title: What is AI literacy? A clear definition for 2026
entry.summary: AI literacy is understanding what AI is, how it works at a basic level, what it can and can't do, and how to use it responsibly. Here's a clear definition, why it matters, and how to build it.

entry.title: How to use ChatGPT: a beginner's guide for 2026
entry.summary: To use ChatGPT well, give it context, a role, a clear task, and the format you want — then refine the first answer. Here's how to start, what to use it for, and the habits that get great results.

entry.title: AI for small business: practical ways to use it in 2026
entry.summary: Small businesses can use AI today to save hours on marketing, customer service, admin, and analysis — no technical team required. Here are the highest-value uses and how to start safely.

entry.title: How to tell if something is AI-generated (text, images & more)
entry.summary: There's no 100% reliable way to detect AI content, but a handful of tells — generic phrasing, factual slips, too-perfect structure, and visual glitches — catch most of it. Here's what to look for.

entry.title: AI vs machine learning vs deep learning: the difference, explained
entry.summary: AI is the broad goal of machines doing smart things; machine learning is the main way we build it; deep learning is a powerful type of machine learning. Here's how they fit together, in plain English.

entry.title: The best AI tools for students in 2026 (and how to use them honestly)
entry.summary: The best AI tools for students help you learn faster — explaining concepts, quizzing you, and checking your work — not do the work for you. Here's the shortlist and how to use them without cheating.

entry.title: The best ChatGPT alternatives in 2026 (and when to use each)
entry.summary: The best ChatGPT alternatives are Claude for writing and code, Gemini inside Google, Perplexity for cited research, and Copilot in Microsoft 365. Here's when each one beats ChatGPT — and when to just stick with ChatGPT.

entry.title: How to use Claude: a beginner's guide for 2026
entry.summary: Claude is an AI assistant that's especially good at writing, long documents, and code. To use it well, give it context, a role, and clear instructions — then iterate. Here's how to start and what it's best for.

entry.title: How to use Gemini: a beginner's guide for 2026
entry.summary: Gemini is Google's AI assistant, built into Docs, Gmail, and Sheets. To use it well, prompt it clearly and lean on its Google integration. Here's how to start and what it's best for.

entry.title: What is RAG (retrieval-augmented generation)? Explained simply
entry.summary: RAG, or retrieval-augmented generation, is a technique that lets an AI look up real information before answering — so it's more accurate and can cite sources. Here's how it works and why it matters.

entry.title: AI for teachers: practical, responsible ways to use it in 2026
entry.summary: Teachers can use AI to cut prep time on lesson plans, feedback, and differentiation — freeing hours for actual teaching. Here are the highest-value uses and how to use it responsibly with students.

entry.title: ChatGPT vs Microsoft Copilot: which should you use in 2026?
entry.summary: ChatGPT is the versatile all-rounder; Microsoft Copilot is AI built into Word, Excel, Outlook, and Teams. If your work lives in Microsoft 365, Copilot wins on integration. Here's the honest comparison.

entry.title: The best Coursiv alternatives in 2026
entry.summary: If you're looking for a Coursiv alternative — usually because you want active practice over guided lessons, or clearer pricing — here are the best options, and how to pick.

entry.title: The best Learnova alternatives in 2026
entry.summary: Looking for a Learnova alternative? If you'd rather build a practice habit than work through courses, here are the best apps for learning AI — and how to choose.

entry.title: How to use Perplexity: a beginner's guide for 2026
entry.summary: Perplexity is an AI answer engine that searches the web and answers with citations. To use it well, ask real questions and check the sources. Here's how to start and what it's best for.

entry.title: Gemini vs Perplexity: which should you use in 2026?
entry.summary: Gemini is Google's versatile AI assistant; Perplexity is an answer engine for research with citations. They're built for different jobs — here's when to use each.

entry.title: The best AI image generators in 2026 (and how to choose)
entry.summary: The best AI image generator depends on what you're making: Midjourney for artistic quality, ChatGPT/DALL·E for ease, Adobe Firefly for commercial-safe work, and more. Here's the shortlist.

entry.title: AI trends in 2026: what's actually changing (and what to do about it)
entry.summary: In 2026, AI is getting more agentic, more multimodal, and woven into everyday tools — while AI fluency becomes a baseline skill. Here's what's really shifting and how to stay ahead.

entry.title: What is DeepSeek? The low-cost AI model, explained
entry.summary: DeepSeek is an AI company known for powerful, low-cost, openly available models — including strong reasoning models that rival pricier rivals. Here's what it is, what it's good at, and what to weigh before using it.

entry.title: ChatGPT vs DeepSeek: which should you use in 2026?
entry.summary: ChatGPT is the polished all-rounder with the biggest ecosystem; DeepSeek offers strong reasoning and coding at much lower cost, with open models. Here's the honest comparison and how to choose.

entry.title: What is Grok? xAI's chatbot, explained
entry.summary: Grok is the AI assistant from xAI, built into X (formerly Twitter), known for real-time access to posts and a more conversational, irreverent style. Here's what it is and what it's good at.

entry.title: AI for real estate: practical ways agents can use it in 2026
entry.summary: Real estate agents can use AI to write listings, follow up with leads, summarize market data, and handle admin — saving hours a week. Here are the highest-value uses and how to stay compliant.

entry.title: How to use AI to write a resume (without sounding like a robot)
entry.summary: AI can help you write a sharper resume in minutes — tailoring it to a job, strengthening bullet points, and beating keyword filters. The key is to guide it with real details and never let it invent anything.

entry.title: We tested AI literacy on 50 people. The average score was 5/10.
entry.summary: We pulled the results from the first 50 completions of our free AI IQ test. The average score was 5.2 out of 10, the median was 5, and 56% of people scored 5 or below. Here's the full breakdown of how AI-literate people actually are — and why most overestimate it.
